From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Electric plucked string instrument

For the magazine, see [*Bass Guitar* (magazine)](/wiki/Bass_Guitar_(magazine) "Bass Guitar (magazine)").

The **bass guitar**, also known as the **electric bass**, is the lowest-pitched member of the [guitar](/wiki/Guitar "Guitar") family. It is similar in appearance and construction to an [electric guitar](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar") but with a longer [neck](/wiki/Neck_(music) "Neck (music)") and [scale length](/wiki/Scale_length_(string_instruments) "Scale length (string instruments)"). The electric bass guitar most commonly has four strings, though five-, six-, and seven-stringed models are also built. Since the mid-1950s, the electric bass guitar has often replaced the [double bass](/wiki/Double_bass "Double bass") in [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") due to its lighter weight, smaller size and easier portability, most models' inclusion of [frets](/wiki/Fret "Fret") for easier [intonation](/wiki/Intonation_(music) "Intonation (music)"), and electromagnetic pickups for [amplification](/wiki/Bass_amplifier "Bass amplifier").

The electric bass guitar is usually tuned the same as the double bass, corresponding to pitches one [octave](/wiki/Octave "Octave") lower than the four lowest-pitched strings of a guitar, typically [E](/wiki/E_(musical_note) "E (musical note)"), [A](/wiki/A_(musical_note) "A (musical note)"), [D](/wiki/D_(musical_note) "D (musical note)"), and [G](/wiki/G_(musical_note) "G (musical note)") (5-string models typically add a low B, and 6-string models typically add a high C). It is played with the fingers, thumb or with a [pick](/wiki/Plectrum "Plectrum").

Because the electric bass guitar is a quiet instrument acoustically, it requires [external amplification](/wiki/Bass_amplifier "Bass amplifier"), generally via [electromagnetic](/wiki/Electromagnetic "Electromagnetic") or [piezo-electric](/wiki/Piezo-electric "Piezo-electric") pickups. It can also be used with [direct input boxes](/wiki/DI_unit "DI unit"), audio interfaces, mixing consoles, computers, or bass-effects processors which offer headphone jacks.

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bass_guitar&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

The *New Grove Dictionary of Music and Musicians* refers to this instrument as an "Electric bass guitar, usually with four heavy strings tuned E1'–A1'–D2–G2."[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESadieTyrrell2001-1) It also defines *bass* as "Bass (iv). A contraction of Double bass or Electric bass guitar." *Mottola's Cyclopedic Dictionary of Lutherie Terms* begins its definition of the instrument as "A bass guitar that produces sound primarily with the aid of electronic devices."[[2]](#cite_note-2) According to some authors the proper term is "electric bass".[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWheeler1978101–102-3)[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvansEvans1977342-4) Common names for the instrument are "bass guitar", "electric bass guitar", "electric bass", and simply "bass".[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaconMoorhouse2016-5)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] and some authors claim that they are historically accurate.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoberts2001References_Appendix-6) A bass guitar whose neck lacks [frets](/wiki/Fret "Fret") is termed a [fretless bass](/wiki/Fretless_bass "Fretless bass").

### Scale

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bass_guitar&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Scale")]

Main article: [Scale](/wiki/Scale_length_(string_instruments) "Scale length (string instruments)")

The scale of a bass is defined as the length of the [vibrating](/wiki/Vibrating "Vibrating") strings between the nut and the bridge saddles. On a modern 4-string electric bass guitar, 30" (76 cm) or less is considered short scale, 32" (81 cm) medium scale, 34" (86 cm) standard scale and 35" (89 cm) long scale.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Long scale is more commonly seen on 5-string basses and above to allow for better resonance of the low B string.

The double bass is "acoustically imperfect" like the [viola](/wiki/Viola "Viola"). For a double bass to be acoustically perfect, its body size would have to be twice as that of a cello rendering it practically unplayable, so the double bass is made smaller to make it playable. The pickups in an electric bass guitar addresses the compromises of a double bass by allowing the low notes to be amplified electronically.

### Pickup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bass_guitar&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Pickup")]

Main article: [Pickup](/wiki/Pickup_(music_technology) "Pickup (music technology)")

Pickups are attached to the body of the guitar beneath the strings. They are responsible for converting the vibrations of the strings into interference of a [magnetic field](/wiki/Magnetism "Magnetism") which is converted into analogous electrical voltage sent as input to an [instrument amplifier](/wiki/Instrument_amplifier "Instrument amplifier").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Strings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bass_guitar&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Strings")]

Main article: [Strings](/wiki/String_(music) "String (music)")

Bass guitar strings are composed of a core and winding. The core is a wire which runs through the center of the string and is made of steel, nickel, or an [alloy](/wiki/Alloy "Alloy").[[9]](#cite_note-9) The winding is a smaller gauge wire wrapped around the core. Bass guitar strings vary by the material and cross-sectional shape of the winding.

Common string variants include roundwound, flatwound, halfwound (groundwound), coated, tapewound and taperwound strings. Roundwound and flatwound strings feature windings with circular and rounded-square cross-sections, respectively, with half-round strings being a hybrid between the two. Coated strings have their surface coated with a synthetic layer while tapewound strings feature a metal core with a plastic winding.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) Taperwound strings have a tapered end where the exposed core sits on the bridge saddle without windings.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The choice of winding has considerable impact on the sound of the instrument, with certain winding styles often being preferred for certain musical genres.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bass_guitar&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: History")]

### 1930s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bass_guitar&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1930s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Paul_tutmarc.jpg/250px-Paul_tutmarc.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paul_tutmarc.jpg)

[Paul Tutmarc](/wiki/Paul_Tutmarc "Paul Tutmarc"), inventor of the modern bass guitar, outside his music store in Seattle, Washington

In the 1930s, musician and inventor [Paul Tutmarc](/wiki/Paul_Tutmarc "Paul Tutmarc") of [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle"), Washington, developed the first electric bass guitar in its modern form, a [fretted](/wiki/Fret "Fret") four-string instrument designed to be played horizontally in a position similar to a standard guitar. The 1935 sales catalog for Tutmarc's company Audiovox featured the "Model 736 Bass Fiddle", a solid body electric bass guitar with four strings, a 30+1⁄2-inch (775-millimetre) scale length, and a single [pickup](/wiki/Pickup_(music_technology) "Pickup (music technology)").[[15]](#cite_note-15) Around 100 were made during this period.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoberts200128–29-16)
Audiovox also sold their "Model 236" bass amplifier.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### 1950s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bass_guitar&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1950s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Fender_%2751_Precision_Bass%2C_FGF_museum.jpg/250px-Fender_%2751_Precision_Bass%2C_FGF_museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fender_%2751_Precision_Bass,_FGF_museum.jpg)

An early [Fender Precision Bass](/wiki/Fender_Precision_Bass "Fender Precision Bass")

In the 1950s, [Leo Fender](/wiki/Leo_Fender "Leo Fender") and [George Fullerton](/wiki/George_William_Fullerton "George William Fullerton") developed the first mass-produced electric bass guitar.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESlogCoryat1999154-18) The [Fender Electric Instrument Manufacturing Company](/wiki/Fender_Electric_Instrument_Manufacturing_Company "Fender Electric Instrument Manufacturing Company") began producing the [Precision Bass](/wiki/Fender_Precision_Bass "Fender Precision Bass"), or P-Bass, in October 1951. The design featured a simple uncontoured "slab" body design (with no edge contours) and a [single coil pickup](/wiki/Single_coil_pickup "Single coil pickup"), both features similar to a [Telecaster](/wiki/Fender_Telecaster "Fender Telecaster"). By 1957, the Precision Bass began to resemble the [Fender Stratocaster](/wiki/Fender_Stratocaster "Fender Stratocaster") with the body edges beveled for comfort and the pickup changed to a separate halves [split coil](/wiki/Humbucker "Humbucker") design.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Fender_Bass_Guitar_Patent.jpg/250px-Fender_Bass_Guitar_Patent.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fender_Bass_Guitar_Patent.jpg)

Design patent issued to Leo Fender for the second-generation Precision Bass

The Fender Bass was a revolutionary instrument for working musicians. In comparison to the [upright bass](/wiki/Upright_bass "Upright bass"), the bass guitar could be easily transported. When amplified, the bass guitar was also much less prone than acoustic basses to [audio feedback](/wiki/Audio_feedback "Audio feedback").[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoberts2001-20) The addition of [frets](/wiki/Fret "Fret") enabled bassists to play in tune more easily than on [upright basses](/wiki/Upright_bass "Upright bass") (the "precision" of the Fender model) and allowed guitarists to more easily adapt to the new instrument.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In 1953, [Monk Montgomery](/wiki/Monk_Montgomery "Monk Montgomery") became the first bassist to tour with the Fender bass, in [Lionel Hampton](/wiki/Lionel_Hampton "Lionel Hampton")'s postwar [big band](/wiki/Big_band "Big band").[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorge199891-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) Montgomery was also possibly the first to record with the electric bass, in a session on July 2, 1953, with [the Art Farmer Septet](/wiki/The_Art_Farmer_Septet "The Art Farmer Septet").[[24]](#cite_note-24) Roy Johnson (with Lionel Hampton), and [Shifty Henry](/wiki/Shifty_Henry "Shifty Henry") (with [Louis Jordan](/wiki/Louis_Jordan "Louis Jordan") and His [Tympany Five](/wiki/Tympany_Five "Tympany Five")), were other early Fender bass pioneers.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESlogCoryat1999154-18) [Bill Black](/wiki/Bill_Black "Bill Black"), who played with [Elvis Presley](/wiki/Elvis_Presley "Elvis Presley") and [James Jamerson](/wiki/James_Jamerson "James Jamerson") switched from upright bass to the Fender Precision Bass around 1957.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBacon2010-25) The bass guitar was intended to appeal to guitarists as well as upright bass players, and many early pioneers of the instrument, such as [Joe Osborn](/wiki/Joe_Osborn "Joe Osborn"), and [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney") were originally guitarists.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoberts2001-20)

Also in 1953, [Gibson](/wiki/Gibson_Guitar_Corporation "Gibson Guitar Corporation") released the first short-scale [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin")-shaped electric bass, the EB-1, with an extendable end pin so a bassist could play it upright or horizontally. In 1958, Gibson released the maple arched-top [EB-2](/wiki/Gibson_EB-2 "Gibson EB-2") described in the Gibson catalog as a "hollow-body electric bass that features a Bass/Baritone pushbutton for two different tonal characteristics". In 1959, these were followed by the more conventional-looking [EB-0 Bass](/wiki/Gibson_EB-0 "Gibson EB-0"). The EB-0 was very similar to a [Gibson SG](/wiki/Gibson_SG "Gibson SG") in appearance (although the earliest examples have a slab-sided body shape closer to that of the double-cutaway [Les Paul](/wiki/Gibson_Les_Paul "Gibson Les Paul") Special). The Fender and Gibson versions used [bolt-on](/wiki/Bolt-on_neck "Bolt-on neck") and [set](/wiki/Set-in_neck "Set-in neck") necks.

Several other companies also began manufacturing bass guitars during the 1950s. [Kay Musical Instrument Company](/wiki/Kay_Musical_Instrument_Company "Kay Musical Instrument Company") began production of the K162 in 1952. Also in 1956, at the German trade fair "Musikmesse Frankfurt", the distinctive [Höfner 500/1](/wiki/H%C3%B6fner_500/1 "Höfner 500/1") viola-shaped bass first appeared, constructed using violin techniques by Walter [Höfner](/wiki/H%C3%B6fner "Höfner"), a second-generation violin luthier.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Due to its use by [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney"), it became known as the "Beatle bass".[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaconMoorhouse2016eBook-27) In 1957, [Rickenbacker](/wiki/Rickenbacker "Rickenbacker") introduced the model 4000, the first bass to feature a [neck-through](/wiki/Neck-through "Neck-through")-body design in which the neck is part of the body wood.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The [Burns London](/wiki/Burns_London "Burns London") Supersound was introduced in 1958.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBacon2010-25)

### 1960s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bass_guitar&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1960s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Gibson_eb3_67.jpg/250px-Gibson_eb3_67.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gibson_eb3_67.jpg)

[Gibson EB-3](/wiki/Gibson_EB-3 "Gibson EB-3")

With the explosion in popularity of [rock music](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") in the 1960s, many more manufacturers began making electric basses, including [Yamaha](/wiki/Yamaha_Corporation "Yamaha Corporation"), [Teisco](/wiki/Teisco "Teisco") and [Guyatone](/wiki/Guyatone "Guyatone"). Introduced in 1960, the [Fender Jazz Bass](/wiki/Fender_Jazz_Bass "Fender Jazz Bass"), initially known as the "Deluxe Bass", used a body design known as an offset waist which was first seen on the [Jazzmaster](/wiki/Jazzmaster "Jazzmaster") guitar in an effort to improve comfort while playing seated.[[29]](#cite_note-JeffOwens-29) The Jazz bass, or J-Bass, features two single-coil pickups.

Providing a more "Gibson-scale" instrument, rather than the 34-inch (864 mm) Jazz and Precision, Fender produced the [Mustang Bass](/wiki/Fender_Mustang_Bass "Fender Mustang Bass"), a 30-inch (762 mm) scale-length instrument.[[30]](#cite_note-30) The [Fender VI](/wiki/Fender_VI "Fender VI"), a 6-string bass, was tuned one octave lower than standard guitar tuning. It was released in 1961, and was briefly favored by [Jack Bruce](/wiki/Jack_Bruce "Jack Bruce") of [Cream](/wiki/Cream_(band) "Cream (band)").[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Gibson introduced its short-scale 30.5-inch (775 mm) [EB-3](/wiki/Gibson_EB-3 "Gibson EB-3") in 1961, also used by Bruce.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The EB-3 had a "mini-humbucker" at the bridge position. Gibson basses tended to be instruments with a shorter 30.5" [scale length](/wiki/Scale_length_(string_instruments) "Scale length (string instruments)") than the Precision. Gibson did not produce a 34-inch (864 mm)-scale bass until 1963 with the release of the [Thunderbird](/wiki/Gibson_Thunderbird "Gibson Thunderbird").[[33]](#cite_note-33)

The first commercial fretless bass guitar was the Ampeg AUB-1, introduced in 1966.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoberts2001125–126-34) In the late 1960s, eight-string basses, with four octave paired [courses](/wiki/Course_(music) "Course (music)") (similar to a 12 string guitar), were introduced, such as the [Hagström](/wiki/Hagstr%C3%B6m "Hagström") H8.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### 1970s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bass_guitar&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 1970s")]

In 1972, [Alembic](/wiki/Alembic_Inc "Alembic Inc") established what became known as "boutique" or "high-end" electric bass guitars.[[36]](#cite_note-36) These expensive, custom-tailored instruments, as used by [Phil Lesh](/wiki/Phil_Lesh "Phil Lesh"), [Jack Casady](/wiki/Jack_Casady "Jack Casady"), and [Stanley Clarke](/wiki/Stanley_Clarke "Stanley Clarke"), featured unique designs, premium hand-finished wood bodies, and innovative construction techniques such as multi-laminate [neck-through-body](/wiki/Neck-thru "Neck-thru") construction and [graphite](/wiki/Carbon_fiber_reinforced_polymer "Carbon fiber reinforced polymer") necks. Alembic also pioneered the use of onboard electronics for pre-amplification and equalization.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Active electronics increase the output of the instrument, and allow more options for controlling tonal flexibility, giving the player the ability to amplify as well as to attenuate certain frequency ranges while improving the overall frequency response (including more low-register and high-register sounds). 1976 saw the UK company [Wal](/wiki/Wal_(bass) "Wal (bass)") begin production of their own range of active basses.[[39]](#cite_note-39) In 1974 [Music Man](/wiki/Music_Man_(company) "Music Man (company)") Instruments, founded by Tom Walker, [Forrest White](/wiki/Forrest_White "Forrest White") and [Leo Fender](/wiki/Leo_Fender "Leo Fender"), introduced the [StingRay](/wiki/Music_Man_StingRay "Music Man StingRay"), the first widely produced bass with active (powered) electronics built into the instrument.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Basses with active electronics can include a [preamplifier](/wiki/Preamplifier "Preamplifier") and [knobs](/wiki/Equalizer_(audio) "Equalizer (audio)") for boosting and cutting the low and high frequencies.

In the mid-1970s, five-string basses, with a very low "B" string, were introduced. In 1975, bassist [Anthony Jackson](/wiki/Anthony_Jackson_(musician) "Anthony Jackson (musician)") commissioned luthier [Carl Thompson](/wiki/Carl_Thompson_(luthier) "Carl Thompson (luthier)") to build a six-string bass tuned (low to high) B0, E1, A1, D2, G2, C3, adding a low B string and a high C string.[[41]](#cite_note-41)
